one_line_takeaway: BiMark is an unbiased, model- and message-agnostic multi-bit LLM watermark whose bit-flip + multilayer + XOR design preserves text quality while beating MPAC's message-extraction rate by up to ~30% on short texts.
key_finding: BiMark delivers substantially higher multi-bit extraction rates than the state-of-the-art model-/message-agnostic baseline MPAC, especially on short texts and long messages, while keeping perplexity lower. On 50-token texts it improves extraction by 20.87% / 29.50% / 30.02% for 8/16/32-bit messages over MPAC, and it stays more robust under synonym substitution (e.g., +18.44% / +21.30% / +26.24% over MPAC at 0.1/0.2/0.3 substitution ratios on 100-token texts). On downstream tasks its scores are statistically indistinguishable from non-watermarked text, unlike the biased Soft Red List which degrades as strength grows.
core_contribution: BiMark is an inference-time, multi-bit watermarking framework for LLM-generated text that simultaneously targets three goals prior methods could not jointly achieve: text-quality preservation, model-agnostic (and message-agnostic) detection, and message-embedding capacity. Its three OWN innovations are: (1) a 'bit-flip' unbiased reweighting function that uses a fair coin flip over a token-counting-based vocabulary bipartition (V0, V1) to symmetrically raise/lower token probabilities, keeping the expected distribution unchanged (Theorem 4.2, E_e[R(P)]=P) while remaining detectable via a simple green-token z-test WITHOUT access to model probabilities; (2) a multilayer reweighting mechanism that composes d independent bipartition/coin-flip layers to boost detectability without breaking the unbiased property, giving d independent votes per token; and (3) an XOR-enhanced position-allocation encoding that combines each selected message bit m[p] with a one-time-pad mask bit (e_i = m[p] XOR b_i), which both makes the message recoverable and guarantees the coin flips stay Bernoulli(0.5) regardless of message content, enabling message-agnostic extraction via a majority-vote voting matrix.
threat_model: Provenance/authenticity setting: a model provider wants LLM-generated text to be reliably distinguishable from human text and to carry embedded metadata (model identity, timestamp, content provenance) to satisfy regulatory mandates (EU AI Act, US executive order). The verifier detects and extracts the watermark using only the secret key, without access to the generating model's architecture, parameters, or the message space (model-agnostic and message-agnostic detection). The adversary is a text modifier: the paper evaluates robustness against synonym-substitution attacks (BERT- and WordNet-based) that alter 10-30% of tokens attempting to remove the watermark while preserving meaning.
limitations: Robustness is evaluated against synonym substitution (main text, Tab. 3) and paraphrasing via DIPPER (Appendix B, Tab. 5), but not against translation round-trips, cropping, or adaptive/spoofing attacks. Under heavy paraphrasing BiMark is NOT uniformly best: at high lexical+order diversity (20,20) SynthID's detection edges it out at 50-, 100-, and 200-token lengths (e.g., 100-token: SynthID 76.13 vs BiMark 70.90), so BiMark's advantage there is over Soft Red List and DiPmark rather than SynthID. The zero-bit comparison (Fig. 4) is reported only as TPR@1%FPR curves, and BiMark is described as merely 'comparable' to SynthID. Detectability is non-monotonic in both layer count and scaling factor (peaks then declines when d or delta_tilde grows too large), so d=10 and delta_tilde=1.0 are empirically tuned rather than principled. Multilayer reweighting adds per-token compute overhead (0.036s at batch 1, 0.047s at batch 50). Extraction rate on the hardest setting (32-bit, 50-token) is only 66.35%, well below reliable, and quantitative multi-bit message-capacity comparisons are limited to MPAC.

quantitative_results: 8-bit, 50-token: BiMark extraction 95.26% @ PPL 8.5 vs MPAC(1) 49.49% @ 9.44 and MPAC(1.5) 78.81% @ 9.76
8-bit, 300-token: BiMark 97.88% @ PPL 4.57 vs MPAC(1.5) 98.57% @ 8.39 (BiMark far lower perplexity)
Extraction-rate gains over MPAC on 50-token texts: +20.87% (8-bit), +29.50% (16-bit), +30.02% (32-bit)
Synonym-substitution robustness (8-bit, 100-token): BiMark +18.44% / +21.30% / +26.24% over MPAC at 0.1 / 0.2 / 0.3 substitution ratios
Downstream (summarization / translation): BiMark BERTScore 32.48 / ROUGE-1 38.32 / MT-BERTScore 56.14 / BLEU 22.15 vs No-Watermark 32.45 / 38.32 / 56.21 / 21.93
Soft Red List degrades with strength: BLEU drops 21.36 (delta=1.0) -> 20.67 (1.5) -> 19.63 (2.0)
Per-token generation cost: 0.036s (batch 1), 0.047s (batch 50); config delta_tilde=1.0, d=10
Under heavy paraphrase (20,20) SynthID beats BiMark at short lengths: 100-tok SynthID 76.13 vs BiMark 70.90; 200-tok SynthID 94.75 vs BiMark 87.28; both far exceed Soft Red List and DiPmark
baselines_compared: MPAC (Yoo et al., 2024) at delta=1 and delta=1.5
Soft Red List (Kirchenbauer et al., 2023) at delta=1.0/1.5/2.0
